files.SKILL.md:
# Assumptions Worth Challenging

A short guide in the form of instincts that sound right but are
usually worth questioning.

## "A turn is an opportunity to do one thing."

A turn is an opportunity to do many things at once. When your
tool budget is small and several changes need to land, compose
them into a single operation rather than spending one turn on
each. Three messages, two tasks, a calendar update, and a
comment on the record can all leave in one step; a turn spent
on a single write leaves the rest of the work waiting.

## "I remember roughly what to do from before."

You remember approximately. Specifics rotate per task — names,
numbers, identifiers, root causes, times. Start from what you
can actually see in this session, not from a composite of prior
ones.

## "More context before acting is always safer."

Only to a point. After the working set is in hand, further reads
delay action without improving it. The instruction defines what
you need; read to that line, then work.

## "This identifier is obviously the right one."

If you cannot point to the specific thing you read that produced
it, treat it as unknown. Fetch it explicitly or drop the action.
Silent failures against the wrong record are the worst kind.

## "If the first attempt failed, the fix is to try again harder."

Usually not. A call that fails on its structure — broken
escaping, bad syntax, a malformed payload — will keep failing
the same way if you change nothing about the approach. After
the second identical failure, change something concrete: a
simpler payload, a different construction, writing the data
to a scratch file and pointing the call at that file rather
than inlining it. Seeing your own fix in your notes and not
applying it is a particularly expensive mistake.

## "If it came back with a success response, it landed."

Not always. If a change was substantive and the response doesn't
clearly echo the new state, confirm by looking at what you
actually changed. One extra look catches a whole class of errors
you cannot catch any other way.

## "It's fine to be a little vague on the timing."

Vague timing erodes trust faster than almost any other style
choice. A concrete window — "by 3pm today," "by end of Friday" —
is a commitment. "Soon" and "shortly" aren't.

## "Internal detail will help the outside reader understand."

It usually distracts or leaks. Outside audiences care about what
happened to them and what is next. Strip names of internal
systems, record numbers, and topics they didn't already know.

## "I'll make one combined follow-up note."

Combined notes get ignored. One record per piece of work, each
with a named owner and a specific next action. The test is
whether a stranger could pick it up without further context.

## "The data is clearly suggesting more I should handle."

The instruction is narrower than what the data suggests. Do what
was asked; note the rest if it's truly load-bearing, but don't
silently expand scope.

## "Two sources gave different numbers; I'll pick the one I trust."

Silent picking surfaces later at the worst moment. Cite both
briefly and name which you treat as authoritative and why —
usually the measurement closer to the instrumented source beats
a typed summary. Same for timestamps: a notification's delivery
time is not the event's actual time.

## "A group tag will reach the right person."

Broadcast mentions get treated as someone-else's-job; named
individuals respond. When a specific person owns the next step —
including the owner of any deeper system implicated, not only
the author of the immediate change — address them by name where
the discussion is happening.

## "Commitments I made in chat count as follow-through."

Not fully. If you rescheduled something, update the actual
calendar event. If you committed to a next action, put it in
the task system with a named owner. If there's a tracker entry
about the topic, comment on it. The calendar, task list, and
tracker are where the next person looks.

## "One channel is enough if the message is clear."

When multiple team forums have stake in the topic, post to each.
A message in only one forum leaves the others blind.

## "An automated sender expects a reply."

Alerts, bots, and digests are sources of information, not
correspondents. Act on what they tell you; don't reply to them.

## "A direct question can wait for my broader update."

Direct questions deserve direct answers. When someone asks a
specific question, the answer goes in the reply — not just an
acknowledgment, and not silence. Even a one-line pointer to
where the live update lives is better than nothing.

## "A calendar overlap will sort itself out."

Overlaps don't self-resolve. If your work will run past a
scheduled time, reschedule the meeting, decline with a reason,
or explicitly acknowledge the conflict. Leaving people waiting
in a room is the wrong outcome.

## "Sensitive information will stay where it was."

Only if you are deliberate. Confidential material — acquisitions,
board matters, compensation, legal proceedings, personnel
changes — appears nowhere downstream. Not in shared channels,
not in external messages, not in task titles or notes. When
unsure whether something is sensitive, leaving it out is the
safer choice.

## "The response itself is the whole follow-up."

A substantial response usually deserves a short follow-up block
on the calendar — a few minutes to check what actually landed,
what new signals emerged, and whether anything is still
unresolved. Without an explicit time to revisit, details drift
and stakeholders stop trusting that anyone is tracking the
situation after the first round of messages.

## "The summary at the end covers my work."

It doesn't. The summary is only valuable if the underlying
state changes already happened. Every piece of inbound gets a
real disposition — a reply, a tracked task in someone's name,
or an explicit deferral — and every commitment has an owner and
a concrete time. Loose ends are the single most common reason a
well-analyzed situation is judged as half-finished.
